full_response:
Below is the step‐by‐step conversion of the curve 
v(λ) = ( x(λ), y(λ), z(λ) ) = ( sin(λ²), ln(λ+1), 2λ²−1 ),   0<λ<1 
into spherical coordinates ( r(λ), θ(λ), φ(λ) ), where by convention  
 r≥0,  
 θ∈[0,π] is the polar angle from the +z–axis, and  
 φ∈[0,2π) is the azimuthal angle in the x–y plane from the +x–axis.

1.  Recall the definitions  
    • r = √[ x² + y² + z² ],  
    • θ = arccos( z / r ),  
    • φ = arctan2( y, x )  (with arctan2 placing φ in the correct quadrant).

2.  Write x(λ), y(λ), z(λ):  
    x(λ) = sin(λ²)  
    y(λ) = ln(λ+1)  
    z(λ) = 2λ² − 1  

3.  Compute r(λ):  
    r(λ) = √{ [sin(λ²)]² + [ln(λ+1)]² + [2λ² − 1]² }  

4.  Compute θ(λ):  
    θ(λ) = arccos(  z(λ) / r(λ) )  
           = arccos( (2λ² − 1)  /  √{ [sin(λ²)]² + [ln(λ+1)]² + [2λ² − 1]² } )  

5.  Compute φ(λ):  
    Since for 0<λ<1 we have sin(λ²)>0 and ln(λ+1)>0, φ lies in the first quadrant and  
    φ(λ) = arctan(  y(λ) / x(λ) )  
           = arctan( ln(λ+1)  /  sin(λ²) )  
    (Or more rigorously φ(λ)=arctan2(ln(λ+1), sin(λ²)).)

Putting it all together:

\boxed{ 
\begin{aligned}
r(λ)&=\sqrt{\sin^2(λ^2)\;+\;\bigl[\ln(λ+1)\bigr]^2\;+\;(2λ^2-1)^2}\,,\\
θ(λ)&=\arccos\!\Bigl(\frac{2λ^2-1}{\sqrt{\sin^2(λ^2)+[\ln(λ+1)]^2+(2λ^2-1)^2}}\Bigr)\,,\\
φ(λ)&=\arctan\!\Bigl(\frac{\ln(λ+1)}{\sin(λ^2)}\Bigr)\,,\qquad 0<λ<1~.
\end{aligned}
}

Confidence: 90%
